summary: Jeff Atwood highlights the common developer trap of 'frameworkitis' - the tendency to procrastinate on actual projects by building elaborate frameworks instead. He shares Alex Gorbatchev's experience of failing to complete a blog engine five times because he kept getting sidetracked building libraries and thinking about future uses. The post suggests that simply writing code that works for the immediate need is more productive than over-engineering. Atwood also wryly notes that writing your own blog software might itself be a form of procrastination.
key_points: Frameworkitis is the habit of procrastinating by developing frameworks instead of shipping working software
Building for hypothetical future uses is a major obstacle to completing projects
Writing code that only does what you need right now helps projects move forward
Even the decision to write your own blog engine might be procrastination disguised as productivity
money_quotes: Every time I have started working on my own blog, I delved deep into creating frameworks and needless to say, none of my previous 5 attempts went even as far as being able to post a new entry.
It really is very hard trying to stay away from creating libraries, thinking about future uses and what extra things I can put in that might used in the future.
Simply creating code that works and only does what I need it to really helps to move things ahead.
I wonder if writing your own blog software isn't a form of procrastination in and of itself.
key_insight: The urge to build reusable frameworks instead of shipping working software is one of the most common ways developers sabotage their own projects.
